FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data with participants aged 9-10 at baseline (n=9,474), longitudinal cohort study with data collected between 2016 and 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time (average hours per week on electronic devices including TV, texting, gaming, social media) and social media use separately. Both qualify as smartphone/social media variables per criteria

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable linear models examined anxiety/depression as outcome with screen time and social media trajectories as predictors. Group-based trajectory models identified patterns, and linear regression tests reported associations with statistical significance

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple p-values and statistics reported: screen time groups showed p≤0.001 for anxiety/depression; social media group p=0.031 for anxiety; p≤0.014 for depression. 95% CIs reported in text (e.g., 95% CI: 0.75, 2.12 for Increasing Use group anxiety)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using ABCD cohort data; group-based trajectory modeling with prospective follow-up from baseline (2016) to year 3 (2022)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Public Health (2025), DOI: 10.1186/s12889-025-25956-3

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply because: (a) paper includes cross-sectional analyses with smartphone/SM as DV; (b) paper reports smartphone/SM → non-SM outcome analyses (screen time and social media trajectories predicting anxiety/depression outcomes); (c) substance use (alcohol/e-cigarette) tested as moderator, representing individual-level predictor beyond demographics
notes: Clear longitudinal smartphone/SM exposure with mental health outcomes. Alcohol/e-cigarette moderation tested but did not reach significance

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD cohort with sample comprising 7,471 young adolescents (mean age 9.87±0.62 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures generic 'screen use' without specifying smartphone or social media. Abstract states 'physical activities, screen use and sleep duration' influenced intelligence but does not clarify screen use as phone/SM-specific. Does not meet qualifying criteria (not smartphone ownership, app use, platform-specific social media, or measured problematic scales)

FT_IC3.evidence: Fails FT_IC2 prerequisite. No separately measured smartphone or social media variable reported with association to non-SM outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric values (β, OR, p-value) provided for screen use associations with intelligence outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD data with linear mixed effect models examining associations between MRI phenotypes and intelligence, including lifestyle factors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Translational Psychiatry (2025), DOI: 10.1038/s41398-025-03467-4

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 not applicable because FT_IC2 is NOT_MET (screen use is not a qualifying smartphone/SM variable)
notes: Paper focuses on neurotransmitter mechanisms and brain phenotypes associated with fluid vs crystallized intelligence. Screen use mentioned in context of lifestyle factors but not as primary exposure variable with extractable coefficients

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study data with 11,875 US adolescents enrolled; 8,125 participants with MRI scans and cognitive tests after quality control

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposures are reading books and television viewing. Per criteria: 'TV viewing alone' explicitly NOT_MET; reading on screens is NOT_MET ('Reading on screens, e-mail, general browsing' excluded). Neither reading nor TV viewing qualify as smartphone or social media variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Fails FT_IC2 prerequisite. Reading and TV viewing are not smartphone/SM variables

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric values for smartphone/SM associations (exposure variables are reading and TV, not phone/SM)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with linear mixed effect models examining associations between reading/TV habits and brain morphology and neurocognitive performance in ABCD sample

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Scientific Reports (2025), DOI: 10.1038/s41598-025-88398-2

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 not applicable because FT_IC2 is NOT_MET (reading and TV viewing are not qualifying smartphone/SM variables per protocol)
notes: Study examines reading vs TV viewing impacts on brain development, both explicitly excluded from smartphone/SM criteria. Title mentions 'television habits' which does not meet inclusion threshold
